region_of_origin: India — Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general buttermilk entry (FOODON_03311737). Neer Mor is a highly diluted, spiced South Indian variant; no exact FoodOn term exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Buttermilk and similar sour milk drinks
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers buttermilk and similar sour milk drinks. Neer Mor is a spiced, heavily diluted variant; this is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for Neer Mor or spiced diluted buttermilk. Null retained to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Neer Mor is one glass of approximately 250 ml, which is the typical portion distributed at street corners and served alongside South Indian meals.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides values for plain buttermilk (thin curd-water preparation). Neer Mor is a spiced, heavily diluted variant. Values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN buttermilk entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, ID 01088, Buttermilk, fluid, cultured, lowfat) to confirm plausibility of the diluted profile. The input enriched_json values are consistent with this adapted estimate and are retained with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for plain low-fat yogurt/buttermilk
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Neer Mor in the University of Sydney GI Database. The closest defensible analogous entry is plain low-fat yogurt / cultured buttermilk, which has a published GI of approximately 15–36 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). Neer Mor is more diluted than plain yogurt, further reducing glycemic impact. A conservative GI of 15 is assigned. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (15 × 1.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 2.5) / 100 = 0.45, rounded to 0.5. This is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Added salt contributes approximately 120 mg sodium per 100 ml (300 mg per 250 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should reduce or omit the added salt when preparing Neer Mor, as a standard salted serving provides approximately 300 mg sodium, which is a meaningful fraction of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive patients.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (1.2 g per 100 ml) with no added sugar and a negligible glycemic load of approximately 0.5 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Neer Mor is an excellent hydration choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycemic load, absence of added sugar, and probiotic content from yogurt, which may support glycemic regulation; however, the sodium content should be monitored if the patient also has hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low total fat (1.0 g per 100 ml) and low saturated fat (approximately 0.6 g per 100 ml), with probiotic content that may support lipid metabolism
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Neer Mor is generally suitable for individuals with cardiovascular disease given its very low fat and calorie content; those on sodium-restricted cardiac diets should prepare it without added salt to keep sodium intake within clinical targets.

lactose_intolerance.key_factor: Dairy-based beverage; fermentation partially reduces lactose but does not eliminate it
lactose_intolerance.guidance: Individuals with severe lactose intolerance should avoid Neer Mor or use lactose-free yogurt as the base; those with mild intolerance may tolerate it due to the partial lactose breakdown during yogurt fermentation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Neer Mor (Tamil: நீர் மோர்; Malayalam: സംഭാരം / Sambharam) is a traditional South Indian summer beverage made by whisking yogurt (curd) with a large quantity of water to produce a very thin, lightly sour drink. The name literally means 'watery buttermilk' in Tamil. It is seasoned with salt and a freshly ground or finely chopped paste of ginger, green chillies, and coriander leaves, and finished with a hot tempering (tadka) of mustard seeds and curry leaves in a small amount of oil or ghee. The result is a pale, frothy, savory drink with a cooling, mildly spiced flavour and a light, watery texture. Neer Mor is deeply embedded in South Indian food culture — it is a sacred offering during the Rama Navami festival and is freely distributed at street corners across Tamil Nadu, Kerala, Karnataka, and Andhra Pradesh during the peak summer months as a public health measure against heat exhaustion and dehydration. Nutritionally, it is extremely low in calories (approximately 18 kcal per 100 ml), low in fat (1.0 g per 100 ml), and low in carbohydrates (1.2 g per 100 ml), making it one of the most calorie-efficient hydration beverages in South Asian cuisine. It provides modest amounts of calcium (35 mg per 100 ml), potassium (45 mg per 100 ml), and riboflavin (0.04 mg per 100 ml) from the yogurt base. The probiotic content of the yogurt base supports gut health and aids digestion, which is why it is traditionally consumed after meals. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), with a negligible glycemic load of 0.5 per 250 ml serving, making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The primary clinical caution is its sodium content from added salt (approximately 120 mg per 100 ml), which is relevant for individuals with hypertension or on sodium-restricted cardiac diets. Preparing Neer Mor without added salt eliminates this concern entirely.

confidence_reason: Nutritional values are adapted from ICMR-NIN buttermilk data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy buttermilk entry. No exact published composition entry for Neer Mor as a named dish exists in either source. The values are internally consistent and plausible for a heavily diluted yogurt-water preparation, but are not derived from a direct laboratory analysis of Neer Mor. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using plain low-fat yogurt/buttermilk as the closest proxy.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Nutritive Value of Indian Foods' buttermilk entry and cross-validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ID 01088 (Buttermilk, fluid, cultured, lowfat). No direct published composition entry for Neer Mor as a named dish exists in either approved source. Values reflect a typical preparation of approximately 1 part yogurt to 3–4 parts water with salt and spices. 2. Saturated fat (0.6 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.2 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.05 g), and cholesterol_mg (3.0 mg) are estimated from the USDA SR Legacy buttermilk entry scaled to the dilution ratio; these are approximate. 3. water_g (96.5 g per 100 g) is estimated based on the high dilution ratio typical of Neer Mor preparation. 4. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 g; this is consistent with a minimally processed dairy-water preparation with no hydrogenated fats. 5. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate using plain low-fat yogurt/buttermilk from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2021). No direct GI measurement for Neer Mor exists in the approved GI database. 6. The input record contained three duplicate portion entries (all '1 serving 250 ml'); only one unique portion is retained in this output. 7. The dietary tag 'low_sodium' in the input is inconsistent with 120 mg sodium per 100 ml (300 mg per 250 ml serving); this tag has been removed from the output classification. The beverage is low in calories and fat but is not low-sodium by standard clinical definitions (low-sodium typically means less than 140 mg per serving). 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy buttermilk data scaled to Neer Mor dilution; detailed fatty acid profile for this specific preparation is not directly available in ICMR-NIN or USDA as a named dish.
